items.title: AI and the new era of credentials theft
items.author: Zack Pediay
items.content:
• Most breaches start with stolen credentials—not advanced hacking—and 80% involve compromised passwords.
  • AI is making account takeover attacks faster, smarter, and harder to detect, even bypassing MFA.
  • Traditional identity-based security isn’t enough; organizations need data-centric, zero-trust defenses.
  • Human risk management tools like Polymer detect and contain compromised accounts before damage spreads.

Despite what Hollywood might suggest, most data breaches don’t involve elite hackers cracking firewalls with lines of code. In reality, all it often takes is one stolen password. Just look at the Samsung breach last month—hackers used compromised credentials to access the personal data of over 270,000 customers.

This kind of incident isn’t rare. In fact, 80% of all hacking-related breaches involve stolen or misused credentials.

And it’s only getting worse.

So, what can you do to safeguard your organization before it’s too late? Let’s take a look.

Account takeover attacks: Why they’re so prevalent

Account takeover attacks are a go-to method for cybercriminals, and it’s easy to see why. On the dark web, millions of stolen passwords are up for grabs, often coming from past data breaches or hacking attempts. The problem? Password reuse. Around 64% of people use the same password across multiple accounts. And even if they don’t reuse the exact same password, it’s often a variation—like tweaking a letter to uppercase or adding an exclamation mark—making it all too easy for attackers to crack.

Even those who take password security seriously aren’t immune. Phishing tactics and the sheer volume of data breaches each year mean that a “unique” password doesn’t stay safe for long. Once one account is compromised, it’s just a matter of time before hackers use those credentials to break into other accounts, especially if users are repeating variations of the same passwords across platforms.

And while multi-factor authentication (MFA) has become a widely adopted security measure to prevent attacks involving stolen credentials, it’s far from foolproof. Cybercriminals have already figured out ways to bypass MFA using social engineering tactics—just look at the Twilio and Uber incidents. In these cases, attackers exploited human vulnerabilities—tricking employees into revealing access to MFA codes.

But this is just the beginning. With the rise of artificial intelligence, the scale and sophistication of these attacks are set to increase dramatically.

A new threat: AI-enabled credentials compromise

AI is making account takeover attacks faster, smarter, and more dangerous than ever before. Cybercriminals are no longer relying on simple password guessing or basic social engineering. They’ve got a powerful new tool in their arsenal.

Here’s how attackers are leveraging AI to facilitate almost undetectable account takeover attacks:

  • Credentials theft: Hackers can buy stolen usernames and passwords from the dark web or exploit old breaches. Once they have these, they turn to AI to test them across multiple platforms. AI agents automate the process, checking stolen credentials against hundreds, even thousands, of sites and apps in seconds.
  • Password cracking: If passwords are weak, AI can accelerate the brute-force attack process. Machine learning models can predict common password patterns with frightening precision, cracking weak passwords far faster than traditional methods.
  • Targeted phishing scams: Cybercriminals can use AI to launch large-scale, hyper-targeted phishing campaigns—creating emails that are eerily convincing and personalized. These emails often lead to sophisticated phishing websites designed to steal MFA codes as easily as usernames and passwords. Even with MFA in place, employees can be tricked into entering their second-factor codes, allowing attackers to bypass that extra layer of security.
  • Business email compromise: Once an attacker has compromised an account, AI helps them continue their deception. By using natural language processing (NLP), AI can analyze an individual’s unique communication style. Armed with a convincing digital impersonation, attackers can escalate the attack—sending eerily convincing fraudulent emails and messages to other employees, requesting data, money, or access to other secure systems.

From identity-centric security to data-centric security

As AI enhances the effectiveness of account takeover attacks, organizations must transition from relying on identity-based security to adopting a data-centric approach. This shift means moving away from the assumption that users granted network access can be trusted by default. Instead, companies must fully embrace a zero-trust mindset, continuously verifying and monitoring user behavior and data access to detect and prevent risky actions.

It may sound bleak to acknowledge that account takeover attacks are inevitable, but that’s the reality we face. Even before AI, attackers have consistently found ways to bypass multi-factor authentication and infiltrate user accounts. The key is to accept that these attacks will happen and ensure you have the right tools in place to stop compromised accounts from causing serious damage.

Enter: Human risk management

This is where human risk management (HRM) solutions come into play. HRM tools are designed to identify, measure, and mitigate insider threats—including compromised accounts. They achieve this by constantly monitoring user interactions with sensitive data across platforms, looking for risky behaviors like unusual download attempts or deviations from normal access patterns.

The best HRM tools go beyond detection and move into action, integrating data security posture management controls to enforce security when a compromised account is detected. These tools apply zero-trust principles to redact sensitive data, restrict access, and immediately flag the security team for investigation.

AI is changing the security landscape—and not in organizations’ favor. Credential-based attacks are getting faster, smarter, and more convincing by the day. Traditional identity and perimeter controls aren’t enough.

To protect against the next wave of breaches, organizations need to assume that account compromise is inevitable—and focus on mitigating the damage when it happens.

Human risk management and data-centric security are your best defense—and Polymer has both. Request a demo to see how Polymer can protect your organization from the next-generation of account takeover attacks.

items.title: A Step-By-Step Guide to Running AgenticSeek Locally: No API Needed
items.author: Aditi Bindal
items.content:
If you've ever imagined having your own Jarvis (Just A Rather Very Intelligent System), an AI that can code, browse the web, plan projects, and even chat with you through voice, all while staying completely offline and private, AgenticSeek is that vision made real. This open-source, fully local alternative to invite dependant AI assistants like Manus AI brings next-gen capabilities directly to your device. On your command, AgenticSeek autonomously chooses the right AI agent, fetches information from the internet, writes or debugs code, fills out web forms, and even builds a plan to execute your task. And it does all this without needing any API usage, and can be operated locally, meaning your data never goes to an external unknown server.

In this guide, we’ll walk you through a comprehensive and step-by-step process to install AgenticSeek locally so you can start experiencing this privacy-first, voice-enabled AI agent today.

The minimum system requirements for this use case are:

  • GPUs: RTX 4090 or RTX A6000 (for smooth execution).

  • Disk Space: 100 GB (may vary across models)

Step-by-step process to install and run AgenticSeek locally

For the purpose of this tutorial, we’ll use a GPU-powered Virtual Machine by NodeShift since it provides high compute Virtual Machines at a very affordable cost on a scale that meets GDPR, SOC2, and ISO27001 requirements. Also, it offers an intuitive and user-friendly interface, making it easier for beginners to get started with Cloud deployments. However, feel free to use any cloud provider of your choice and follow the same steps for the rest of the tutorial.

Step 1: Setting up a NodeShift Account

Visit app.nodeshift.com and create an account by filling in basic details, or continue signing up with your Google/GitHub account.

If you already have an account, login straight to your dashboard.

Step 2: Create a GPU Node

After accessing your account, you should see a dashboard (see image), now:

1) Navigate to the menu on the left side.

2) Click on the  option.

3) Click on to start creating your very first GPU node.

These GPU nodes are GPU-powered virtual machines by NodeShift. These nodes are highly customizable and let you control different environmental configurations for GPUs ranging from H100s to A100s, CPUs, RAM, and storage, according to your needs.

Step 3: Selecting configuration for GPU (model, region, storage)

1) For this tutorial, we’ll be using RTX A6000 GPU, however, you can choose any GPU of your choice as per the "Prerequisites".

2) Similarly, we’ll opt for 200GB storage by sliding the bar. You can also select the region where you want your GPU to reside from the available ones.

Step 4: Choose GPU Configuration and Authentication method

1) After selecting your required configuration options, you'll see the available VMs in your region and according to (or very close to) your configuration. In our case, we'll choose a 1x RTX A6000 48GB GPU node with 48 vCPUs/84GB RAM/200 GB SSD.

2) Next, you'll need to select an authentication method. Two methods are available: Password and SSH Key. We recommend using SSH keys, as they are a more secure option. To create one, head over to our official documentation.

The final step would be to choose an image for the VM, which in our case is , where we’ll deploy and run the inference of our model.

That's it! You are now ready to deploy the node. Finalize the configuration summary, and if it looks good, click to deploy the node.

Step 6: Connect to active Compute Node using SSH

1) As soon as you create the node, it will be deployed in a few seconds or a minute. Once deployed, you will see a status in green, meaning that our Compute node is ready to use!

2) Once your GPU shows this status, navigate to the three dots on the right, click on , and copy the SSH details that appear.

As you copy the details, follow the below steps to connect to the running GPU VM via SSH:

1) Open your terminal, paste the SSH command, and run it.

2) In some cases, your terminal may take your consent before connecting. Enter ‘yes’.

3) A prompt will request a password. Type the SSH password, and you should be connected.

Step 7: Set Up Project Environment

Make sure you have Anaconda installed and set up in the system, then start by creating a virtual environment with conda.

conda create -n agenticseek python=3.11 -y && conda activate agenticseek

2) Install Ollama with dependencies.

apt install pciutils lshw
curl -fsSL https://ollama.com/install.sh | sh

3) Start the Ollama server.

4) Open another terminal, connect to remote server and install .

ollama run deepseek-r1:70b

5) Check if model is installed.

Step 8: Clone AgenticSeek & Install Project Dependencies

1) Clone the official repo of the agent.

git clone --depth 1 https://github.com/Fosowl/agenticSeek.git

2) Install all the project requirements.

3) Install other dependencies that are not mentioned in the above script but necessary for the model to run successfully.

apt-get install libportaudio2
conda install -c anaconda pyaudio

Step 9: Configure and Start the Agent Server

Once everything is installed, we'll connect our local VSCode editor to the remote server and write the Python code. We'll then use this code to configure model and edit the code if needed before running it for inference.

If you're using a GPU through a remote server (e.g., NodeShift), you can connect it to your visual studio code editor by following the steps below:

a) Install the “Remote-SSH” Extension by Microsoft on VS Code. b) Type “Remote-SSH: Connect to Host” on the Command Palette.

c) Click on "Add a new host".

d) Enter the host details, such as username and SSH password, and you should be connected.

1) Edit the contents of config.ini as shown below to match the ollama provider.

Create a rough workspace to save all the work files that Agent will produce during inference and place the path of that directory in work_dir.

2) Move inside directory and install the requirements.

pip3 install -r requirements.txt

3) Start the Agent server at port 5000.

python3 app.py --provider ollama --port 5000

Step 10: Set Up Client Machine

Our agent is successfully up and running. Now, its the time to setup client system with backend and frontend interface from where we'll chat with the Agent and obtain the response.

Repeat the in the local system (not inside the remote server) to set up the project in this system, as our local system will work as the client side.

1) Edit the contents of file to point to the remote server for making agent calls.

Create a rough workspace to save all the work files that Agent will produce during inference and place the path of that directory in work_dir.

2) Make sure all the routes mentioned in the contain the "" prefix.

3) Start all the required frontend services inside the Docker desktop.

Move inside the agenticSeek directory and then run this command.

Our frontend services have started successfully, let's move on to start the backend server.

4) If you're on a remote machine (e.g., NodeShift GPU), you'll need to do SSH port forwarding in order to make the agent server accessible for the backend server to make calls to.

Run the following command in your local terminal after replacing:

with the PORT allotted to your remote server (For the NodeShift server - you can find it in the deployed GPU details on the dashboard).

with the path to the location where your SSH key is stored.

with the IP address of your remote server.

ssh -L 5000:localhost:5000 -p <YOUR_SERVER_PORT> -i <PATH_TO_SSH_KEY> root@<YOUR_SERVER_IP>

5) Logout of Hugging Face to remove any expired credentials.

6) Export .

export  SEARXNG_BASE_URL="http://127.0.0.1:8080"

7) Start backend API server.

Step 11: Access the Agent Web Interface

Access the interface at . It'll look something like this:

We'll ask it to find top cafes in Rennes, France.

As shown above, Agent has responded that the creation of the file is successful. Let's see the created file in our local system.

As you can see, it has created the file successfully.

And has mentioned the cafe names as instructed.

By now, you’ve seen how AgenticSeek combines powerful features like autonomous web browsing, intelligent task planning, and take actions like file creation, editing and much more, all while running 100% locally to ensure your data stays private. If you're building projects, researching online, or just experimenting with cutting-edge AI, AgenticSeek helps you without relying on any API or other restrictions. And for developers looking to deploy or test AgenticSeek in a scalable yet private environment, NodeShift cloud offers the perfect solution, giving you full control over your compute while preserving local-first principles. It's the ideal way to blend privacy with performance in your AI workflows.

For more information about NodeShift:

items.title: Comparing Text Search Strategies pg_search vs. tsvector vs. External Engines
items.author: Rishi Raj Jain
items.content:
Choosing the Right Search Approach for Your Application with PostgreSQL and Neon

When implementing search in your application, you need to choose the right text search approach. This guide compares PostgreSQL's built-in , the extension, and external search engines to help you select the best option for your needs.

Built-in PostgreSQL for Text Search

PostgreSQL includes native full-text search capabilities using the data type and search expressions. The data type is a specialized data structure that PostgreSQL uses to represent documents in a form optimized for search.

This built-in functionality works for basic search needs without requiring any additional extensions. It's perfect for smaller applications or when you don't require advanced search features.

To use , you need to create a table with a column to store the search vector. You can then convert text into the format and create an index for efficient searching. This allows your queries to run faster, even as your dataset grows.

This example shows how to:

  • Create a table with a column to store search vectors
  • Convert text to the format using the function
  • Create a GIN index to speed up searches
  • Search for articles containing both "database" and "performance"

While meets basic search needs, it has limitations with relevance ranking, handling typos, and complex search patterns. It’s suitable for small to medium-sized datasets where advanced features aren’t necessary.

  • : No need for any extensions, just native PostgreSQL functionality.
  • : Works seamlessly within the same database, avoiding the need for additional services.
  • : Since is part of Postgres, it doesn’t require a separate system to maintain.
  • Limited relevance ranking: It doesn't automatically rank search results based on relevance.
  • : Exact matches are required, so if a user misspells a search term, it won’t return relevant results.
  • : Handling more complex queries like fuzzy matching or phrase proximity is not possible out-of-the-box.

Extending with

The extension builds on PostgreSQL's search capabilities by adding better relevance ranking with the BM25 algorithm, fuzzy matching for handling typos, and more flexible search options.

This extension is particularly useful for applications that require more sophisticated search features without needing a separate search engine.

With , you get features such as:

  • : The BM25 algorithm provides an automatic ranking of search results based on how relevant they are to the query.
  • : It allows for typo tolerance, meaning searches will return relevant results even when a user makes small errors.
  • : You can search for exact phrases or words that are close together, which can be especially useful in content-heavy applications.
  • : supports more complex search expressions like partial word matches, stemming, and stop-word filtering.

is currently only available on Neon projects created in an AWS region.

Adding to your Neon database is simple - just run this single SQL command:

That's all it takes to enable the extension. You can then use the features in your queries. The setup is straightforward, and you don’t need any additional infrastructure to get started.

Example of Using

After enabling the extension, you can create search indexes and run more sophisticated queries:

  • Create a BM25 index that covers multiple columns
  • Perform a basic keyword search using the operator
  • Find results even when the search term has typos
  • Sort results by relevance so the most relevant results appear first

Using on Neon gives you:

  • Better search capabilities: With ranking, typo tolerance, and complex query options, provides much more functionality than .
  • A fully managed PostgreSQL experience: You don’t need to set up or maintain a separate search service. Neon handles everything for you, from scaling to backups.
  • : Since the search index is part of your PostgreSQL database, there’s no need to worry about synchronizing data between separate systems.
  • : With running on Neon, you avoid the complexity of managing an external search engine while still getting advanced search features.

is a great choice for applications that need more advanced search features but want to avoid the complexity of managing a separate search engine.

External Search Engines (e.g., Elasticsearch)

External search engines like Elasticsearch provide specialized search features for complex use cases and very large datasets. These engines are designed to scale out across many servers and handle high-performance, low-latency search queries.

While these engines offer powerful capabilities, they come with trade-offs:

  • You need to set up and maintain additional infrastructure: External search engines require managing separate servers or cloud services, which can increase operational overhead.
  • You must keep your database and search index synchronized: Ensuring that your external search engine stays in sync with your PostgreSQL database can introduce complexity, especially as your data changes.
  • : Managing another system means additional configuration, monitoring, and troubleshooting.
  • : Running an external search engine comes with extra costs, both in terms of infrastructure and developer time.

External search engines like Elasticsearch provide powerful features such as:

  • Distributed search: Handles large-scale search queries across many machines.
  • Complex querying: Offers advanced querying capabilities such as aggregations, nested fields, and more.
  • Real-time indexing: Updates search results in real time as new data is indexed.

Use Cases for External Search Engines

You might choose an external search engine when:

  • You have extremely large datasets (billions of records) that require distributed search across multiple servers.
  • Your search needs include specialized features not available in PostgreSQL, such as advanced analytics, geographic search, or machine learning integration.
  • to manage additional infrastructure and complexity.
Separate system to install, configure and maintain
Additional infrastructure costs
Requires ongoing maintenance

Which Option Should You Choose?

With all these options available, how do you choose the right one for your application? Here are some guidelines:

Use built-in when:

  • You have simple search requirements
  • Your dataset is small to medium-sized
  • You want to use only built-in PostgreSQL features

Use on Neon when:

  • You need better search relevance and typo tolerance
  • You want to avoid setting up separate systems
  • You prefer a managed database experience
  • Your search needs are important but don't require specialized features

Consider external search engines when:

  • You have extremely large datasets (billions of records)
  • Your search needs include specialized features not available in PostgreSQL
  • You have the resources to manage additional infrastructure

For most web applications, content sites, and e-commerce platforms, on Neon provides a great balance between search features and simplicity. It extends PostgreSQL's capabilities without requiring you to manage separate systems or synchronize data.

When choosing a search strategy, start with the simplest option that meets your needs. For many applications, on Neon offers a great middle ground - better search features than native PostgreSQL without the complexity of a separate search system.

Selecting the right search approach allows you to provide good search functionality to your users while keeping your application architecture as simple as possible.

items.title: Quack The Code - Amazon Q - Making Technology More Accessible
items.author: Mandar Risbud
items.content:
This project tries to make technology accessible to many more people. Today, a lot of developers are good in programming & technology but not many are proficient in English. This becomes a barrier for them to use technology.

Using Amazon Q Developer, I have demonstrated a simple use of Amazon Q to generate documentation in regional languages like Hindi.

Having documentation in familiar, non-English, local languages will make technology accessible to many more people, thus becoming more inclusive. Adoption of Amazon Q will also increase.

How I Used Amazon Q Developer

Here is how I used Amazon Q Developer:

  1. Open new chat in Amazon Q Developer
  2. Give a new "/dev" command and provide following prompt:

a) From Java code file (Neocontroller.java), pick up all routes b) Create a new markdown (.md) file named api.md

c) In this file, add each of the API routes and against each, write in Hindi the description of what that route does.

After this, Amazon Q Developer correctly generates a markdown file with description for each route in Hindi.

Paste this file in a Docusaurus project and you have documentation of API (or your application screens) in non-English, regional language !

items.title: How AI And ML Integration In Your Fitness App Can Boost Business ROI?
items.author: Victoria Scott
items.content:
The fitness app market is expected to hit more than $20 billion in the next 5 years, and a major chunk of the credit goes to AI and ML-driven personalized user experiences.

To launch one of the best fitness applications, you will need smart technology that continuously keeps on learning from its users and adapts according to their needs. This will not only engage your users but will also help you stay ahead of the competitors.

This is where AI in app development, more specifically AI in mobile app development, comes into play.

Integrating Machine Learning and AI into your app will not only modernize your app but will elevate its core values and enhance features like engagement automation and monetization opportunities.

In this blog, I will help you understand how you can incorporate AI-driven enhancements into your fitness apps to keep your users hooked, and eventually boost your ROI.

1. How AI/ML Can Enhance Your Fitness App Value Proposition? How to use AI in an app can be a recurring thought when you are planning to integrate AI in mobile app development. The following section will provide you with a comprehensive idea of how integrating AI in app development can help your app scale seamlessly.

  • Modernizing Core Features

Traditional fitness apps focused mostly on static content like generic workouts and fixed meal plans for everyone, irrespective of their dietary requirements.

But with time, users’ definition of the best fitness application has evolved. They want personalized content and customized feedback to boost their motivation during lengthy workouts. They look for personalization, customization, and tailored feedback.

Integrating AI in app development facilitates all these requirements. Some of the best app development companies allow you to incorporate AI in mobile app development, as it can help you with analysing user behaviour in real time. For example, AI in mobile app development will allow you to reschedule the whole workout routine if a user misses a leg day.

In this way, AI in app development becomes a digital assistant as well as an accountability partner.

To stand out in the saturated digital health tech market, App development companies need to integrate intelligent services as they are no longer a luxury feature but a basic necessity.

AI and ML integration help to add features like voice-activated coaching to movement recognition, and personalized feedback, which makes your app rank among the best fitness applications. They also serve as unique selling points, which are hard to beat.

That's why more businesses are collaborating with a Fitness app development company as they help them build intelligent technologies and hence help in representing them as a technologically sound and forward-thinking organization.

  • Driving User Retention Through Personalization

1.Personalized Workout Plans Using ML algorithms, some of the best fitness application can now serve hyper-personalized workout routines. These are customized according to a user’s personal needs, goals, body type, and nutritional needs.

These personalized plans adapt automatically according to feedback and learning through user performance.

The best app development companies will determine how to use AI in an app development lifecycle so that your app can learn from the user habits and reshape their workout plans in real time.

2.Adaptable Nutrition Guidance AI and ML can personalize your users’ meal plans according to their daily calorie demands and restrictions.

Integrating AI in mobile app development can enhance the holistic health experience that users demand from the best fitness application.

These gains can directly boost ROI for your business if you are collaborating with a fitness app development services provider.

- Smart Coaching and Virtual Assistants

1.AI-Powered Chatbots and Virtual Trainers AI in app development allows integration of a virtual assistant to provide you with 24/7 support. This includes complete solutions from workout tips to injury prevention and first aid tips.This integration of AI in app development is an innovative game-changer; it not only answers questions of the users but also continuously engages with users based on their data.

These features can be a key in developing a fitness brand. These features can also be a good option for a fitness app development company, which aims to provide scalable, low-cost solutions that can enhance user engagement without increasing human intervention.

With interactive AI chatbots, users feel supported and taken care of, which helps them to spend more time on the app and reduces churn rates.

2.Voice and Gesture Recognition Fitness app development services from the best fitness app development company allow you to integrate features like voice commands and gesture recognition. This can aid your user for a hands-free interaction.

This accessibility boosts user experience during workouts.

With intelligent automation features, AI in mobile app development simplifies UI and increases real-time responsiveness. Collaborating with the right app development company confirms that these tools are integrated seamlessly for effortless scalability.

  • Predictive Analysis For Better User Engagement

1. Interaction and Churn Rate Prediction The most valuable benefit of integrating AI in the app development process is its ability to carry out . It can predict user interaction and churn rates. Hence, it becomes able to draw out a predicted pattern of user behaviour.

For example, a drop in frequency of usage or incomplete goals. It can also keep track of trigger retention methods like push notifications and email nudges.

A forward-thinking fitness app development company will make sure to build these predictive analysis models into your app, hence allowing proactive app engagement.

2.Dynamic Content Adjustments

Integrating AI in app development services can also help in suggesting **changes to the app interface or shuffling routines **based on what's receiving the most engagement. These subtle improvements are essential in creating the best fitness application and catching the attention of users.

- AI Insight-Based Feature Evolution

Instead of vague guessing on which feature to launch next, integrated AI in mobile app development can help you recognise and target the pain points of the user. This will allow you to launch with confidence regarding the feature’s market viability.

This data-based statistical approach can also help any fitness app development company to create the best fitness application in its specific field.

Now that we have briefly understood how to use AI in an app, let us see how it can revolutionize the optimization of the revenue generation process.

2.Revenue Optimization Through AI/ML Integration

AI in app development can help with In-app purchases, upselling, and subscription management. AI can identify the best points to offer in app premium purchases and upgrades, based on the user’s personalized data. This makes your user feel catered to a range of premium choices of services rather than being pushed to buy them.

A fitness app development company offering the best fitness app development services can employ ML to predict possible user churn rates. ML, along with AI in mobile app development, can help offer them custom plans that help retain them.

AI in app development can align your monetization strategies with your user behavior patterns. This is exactly how accessing smart fitness app development services can boost your business ROI.

3. Key Challenges of Integrating AI in Mobile App Development

Implementing AI in mobile app development comes with its own set of careful considerations and challenges. Partnering with an experienced Fitness app development company can allow you to carefully navigate through data privacy regulations and ethical concerns regarding transparency in AI decision-making.

By collaborating with a fitness app development services provider, you can ensure that you build the best fitness application that is both compliant and technically sound.

4. How to Choose The Right Fitness App Development Company Collaborating with the right fitness app development company can determine the success trajectory of your application. There are certain key factors that need to be considered while choosing a partner that can turn your vision of building the best fitness application into reality.

- Proven Industry Experience

  • Always look for a fitness app development company that has a proven track record of building the best fitness application.

- AI and ML Integration Capabilities

  • If you have envisioned to leverage AI in app development, make sure your chosen company has a development team that has enough experience with technologies ike meachine learning and predictive analytics.

- Cross-Platform Expertise- The company should offer development services for both Android and iOS platforms, so you get to cater to a broad spectrum audience.

- Scalability and Support

- Client Reviews and References

  • You should always look out for the rankings of app development companies and read user reviews on platforms like Clutch and Good Firms.

Collaborating with the best fitness app development company can give you access to top-notch fitness app development services. This can help you maximize your business’s ROI and gain a competitive edge over your competitors.

ML and AI in app development can offer revolutionary opportunities to turn your fitness app from a mere static tool to a digital wellness partner and a virtual 24/7 nutritional coach. Be it personalizing the content according to your users’ needs or predicting the usage pattern, or automating engagement through chatbots, AI in mobile app development is the optimum solution for boosting ROI.

Every stage in the lifecycle of an app, from onboarding of the user to their engagement and retention, can benefit from the addition of AI in the app development process.

If you are a fitness brand looking to scale your app, collaborating with a fitness app development company should be your primary step in the journey of building the best fitness application.

Investing in a fitness app development company and building a smart and self learning fitness application can lead to building more profitable business solutions for the coming times!

items.title: Which LLM writes the best SQL?
items.author: Cameron Archer
items.content:
We asked 19 popular LLMs (+1 human) to write analytical SQL queries to filter and aggregate a 200 million row dataset. The result is the first version of the LLM SQL Generation Benchmark.

Using a set of 50 analytical questions inspired by this list from maintainers of ClickHouse®, we measure how well each model can write accurate and efficient SQL. We benchmark success rates, exactness, efficiency, query latency, and other metrics, comparing them to queries produced by an experienced human engineer.

The dataset, which contains 200 million rows of public GitHub events data (sampled from the GH Archive), is hosted inTinybird, allowing us to run all the queries interactively and measure performance at scale. The full dashboard with results is public here. We will continually update this dashboard as new models are developed and tested (Want us to test a model? Create an issue or run the test yourself and submit a PR with new results here).

With features like Forward and Explorations, Tinybird is making LLMs a core part of the product experience. That means our users are directly affected by the quality of model-generated SQL, both in correctness and performance.

We built this benchmark to:

  • Continuously evaluate how models behave in real, messy data scenarios.
  • Catch regressions or silent failures as models evolve.
  • Understand tradeoffs between accuracy, latency, and cost.
  • Guide how we prompt, post-process, or choose models in production.

In essence, we're making sure LLMs actually work for SQL generation in the real world. Spoiler: it's not great… yet.

The Dataset: GitHub Archive

The GitHub Archive, a public dataset with all GitHub events since 2011, serves as source data for the benchmark. The archive contains billions of rows tracking stars, forks, issues, PRs, comments, pushes, etc.

We have randomly sampled a 200 million row subset of the original dataset for the benchmark. The data is stored in Tinybird as a single MergeTree table with this schema. It includes a mix of enums, low-cardinality strings, timestamps, and arrays.

Each LLM must produce SQL from 50 natural language prompts or questions about public GitHub activity. You can find the whole list of questions here.

Each question is phrased like a natural-language prompt: “Top 10 repositories by stars over the past 7 days”, etc.

  • Write correct SQL (valid syntax, correct columns, correct grouping)
  • Run it against the table and return accurate output

Each model receives the same system prompt. It tells the model exactly what to do and what not to do, for example:

  • "You are not allowed to hallucinate fields. If a field is not in the schema, the query is invalid."
  • "Do not select all columns. Avoid SELECT * unless explicitly required."
  • "LIMIT without ORDER BY is not allowed."
  • Return only SQL. No explanations.
  • Optimize for performance: minimize .
  • The full table schema is included in the prompt (via inlined schema)
  • A full list of available fields and compatible functions is provided (, , , etc).

So far, we have tested 19 models from Anthropic, OpenAI, Google, Meta, DeepSeek, and Mistral, including:

  • (Anthropic)
  • (OpenAI)
  • (paid and free)

Over time, we intend to expand our testing and will include new models by the major providers as they are released.

How We Measure Performance

Each query is scored using these metrics:

% of questions that returned valid, executable SQL code
% of questions where a valid query was returned on first try
SQL execution latency (ms)
Time the model took to generate a response
How many retries were needed for valid SQL to be generated
How many rows were read per query executed
How much data (in bytes) was read per query executed

In addition to raw performance metrics, we compute a few derived scores to better capture overall quality. These scores combine correctness and efficiency into interpretable values:

How good is an LLM at generating a query, based on how fast that query is generated, how few attempts are needed, and how fast does the query execute? (more below)
How close is the output of that query to the human equivalent? (more below)
(Efficiency + Exactness) / 2

These derived metrics help highlight tradeoffs: some models are precise but inefficient, others fast but error-prone.

Measuring Output Efficiency

An important consideration when asking an LLM to generate analytical SQL queries is how efficient they are with compute resources. In ad hoc analytics and business intelligence scenarios, the impact is likely negligible and worth the tradeoff in human resource time.

However, in real-time, high-concurrency scenarios where queries may be run thousands or millions of times per day, inefficient queries compound and can consume significant compute resources at great expense or UX degradation.

We calculate an efficiency score, which considers both how efficient an LLM is in generating a query (number of retries, total generation time, failures) as well as how efficient the query itself is in terms of rows/data read, bytes per row, and latency. (which is then normalized to a 0-100 scale, with 100 being most efficient).Based on our benchmark results, it seems LLMs can often produce queries that are accurate OR efficient, but less often produce queries that are both accurate AND efficient, especially as prompt complexity increases. 

Some of this may be explained by the system prompt: we ask LLMs to minimize the amount of data read, which may influence their willingness to refine a query until it is accurate if that means making the query less efficient (though we have not tested this assumption with different prompt variations).

Some LLMs seem to generate accurate SQL at the expense of efficiency: either they make multiple attempts or produce queries that read considerably more data than those written by a human. For example, OpenAI o4-mini-high managed to produce functional SQL for all 50 prompts, but on average read 32% more data than a human, the highest of all models.

In other cases, LLMs write efficient queries, but they don't answer the prompt. For example, Meta llama-4-scout seems to write efficient queries in terms of data processing, but in many cases, it either failed to produce a valid query or wrote a functional query that did not address the prompt. That is, the queries are "inexact"…

Measuring Output Exactness

When comparing the output of LLM-generated queries to human-written ones, the challenge isn’t just checking whether the query — it’s whether it returns semantically similar results. But since the shape and order of SQL results can vary (e.g., column names or positions don’t matter), traditional exact matching breaks down, and a more heuristic approach must be taken.

Instead, we treat query results as and measure their similarity using set-based and numeric distance metrics:

  • (0 = identical, 1 = no overlap): compares results as sets of unique rows, ignoring order and column names.
  • (0 = identical, 1 = maximally divergent): compares numeric values in matching rows, accounting for relative error.
  • (higher is better): balances precision and recall between both result sets to capture partial correctness.

To ensure fairness, we apply the same metrics across all questions and models. We don’t adapt thresholds or swap distance functions on a per-case basis. This avoids cherry-picking and makes model comparisons meaningful, even if the numerical distance scale varies across queries.

Each query returns a set of booleans (, , ) and raw distance values. These allow us to track both (pass/fail) and the result is — which is useful when the query is “almost right.”

You'll notice that even the highest-scoring model in terms of exactness, Anthropic's claude-3.7-sonnet, still only scores slightly more than 56 out of 100, on average, in our heuristic measure of exactness.

Many models did fine on correctness but missed huge optimization opportunities. Others frequently failed on correctness while still producing technically efficient queries. Here are the most relevant takeaways from the benchmark, broken down by model group and ranked performance.

Claude dominates accuracy (but not speed)

  • Claude 3.7 ranks #1 overall. Claude 3.5 is #3.
  • Both score 100% valid queries and over 90% generation on first attempt.
  • Average exactness is relatively high (~52–56), but not perfect.
  • LLM generation time is slower (~3.2s).
  • Rows read: ~37M–40M — not the worst, but not exceptionally efficient.

OpenAI’s o3/o4 models are solid all-rounders

  • o3-mini ranks #2. o4-mini is close behind at #6.
  • 100% valid queries, ~88–92% first attempt rate.
  • Rows read: ~49M–52M — competitive, but not top-tier.

LLaMA 3 fails, LLaMA 4 recovers

  • LLaMA 3.3 70B is dead last (#19). Only 66% valid queries.
  • Weak 52% first attempt rate.

  • LLaMA 4 Maverick (#9) and Scout (#11) do much better. Validity: 100% and 96%.

  • Scout reads the least data overall: 129MB/query.

Gemini drops due to latency

  • Gemini 2.5 Pro Preview (#12) is accurate (91.8%) but : 40s/gen.
  • Flash versions (2.0 and 2.5) have faster response times.
  • But lower exactness (~40–42) and weaker scores overall.

Humans still lead in efficiency

  • Human SQL reads ~31M rows and just 760MB per query.
  • No model gets close on raw cost or selectivity.
  • Most models read 1.5x–2x more rows.

LLMs are good at writing SQL, but getting the SQL (that is, the SQL that answers the prompt) is another story. Even when a query runs and returns data, that doesn’t mean it’s semantically correct, efficient, or trustworthy. These are the kinds of edge cases that matter in real-world analytics — and where human judgment still wins.

Correctness is not binary

Just because a query runs doesn't mean it's correct. Models often generate queries that return , but the logic is flawed: incorrect filters, incorrect grouping, or the use of the wrong timestamp. These mistakes are subtle and hard to catch without knowing the intent.

For “top 10 labels related to bugs or features,” a human typically uses and filters with ILIKE '%bug%' OR ILIKE '%feature%'. Most models either skip the arrayJoin or match labels exactly, missing entries like bugfix or feature-request.

This benchmark manually validates outputs. High success rates don’t always mean semantically correct results.

Natural language often isn’t expressive enough for precise data logic. Prompts like “most active repos” or “top contributors” are vague. Do we count issues, PRs, comments, or stars? Over what time range? Using what filters?

LLMs can guess (and often guess well), but if you can’t write or read the query yourself, you can’t verify its correctness. SQL remains a necessary skill.

LLMs struggle with context

Even when models have access to the full schema, they miss contextual details. For instance, issue numbers aren't globally unique, and includes both org and project. These are easy to misinterpret if you don't fully understand GitHub’s event model.

Some LLMs collapse all events under shared fields or reuse identifiers across different contexts. That leads to incorrect joins, inflated counts, or silently wrong aggregations.

Real-time analytics ≠ Text-to-SQL

These are different problems. Real-time systems like Tinybird prioritize performance under load: latency, throughput, and columnar efficiency. LLMs mostly ignore this unless specifically directed.

For “top 10 repositories by issue comments,” a human filters event_type = 'IssueCommentEvent' and aggregates per repo. Some models try to use and rely on the static comments field, which only reflects the current issue state (not the full event stream). Others overcomplicate things with joins that don’t make sense.

These queries aren't just inefficient: they’re wrong in any time-based or streaming context.

When uncertain, LLMs tend to overdo it: reading more rows, selecting more fields, or broadening filters. That bloats resource usage and warps results.

Instead of filtering for 4–5 specific event types, a model might count contributors across all events, including forks, releases, and even gists. That leads to noisy, inflated outputs.

Humans tend to be more conservative. Even if they undercount, the logic is easier to verify and control.

Data exposure is a tradeoff

The best model results come when the model has access to real data. But doing that exposes private or sensitive information, especially when querying with user-level or auth-bound fields.

There’s no real solution yet. This is still an active area of research, and a growing concern in multi-tenant platforms that want to use LLMs safely.

If you're building analytics tools with LLMs in the loop, some recommendations:

  1. - Provide database structure, table relationships, and data types as context to help the LLM generate accurate queries and understand data limitations.
  2. Implement query linting and validation - Add post-processing to check generated SQL for syntax errors, performance issues, or security vulnerabilities before execution.
  3. Balance penalties for inefficiency, inexactness, and gen time based on context - Your use case determines whether query efficiency, exactness, or generation speed is more important. 
  4. Build feedback loops with execution results - Capture query outcomes and error messages to iteratively improve generated queries when initial attempts fail.
  5. - Use different LLMs for different parts of the analytical pipeline (query generation, visualization suggestion, result interpretation).
  6. Add guardrails with structured output formats - Use markup tags to control response structure, ensuring consistent parsing for downstream processes.
  7. Implement self-evaluation checks - Have the model assess its own analytical outputs against defined criteria before presenting results.
  8. Create context-aware prompt templates - Develop standardized templates that incorporate user history, query patterns, and domain-specific constraints.

Explore the benchmark, contribute, and suggest models

The dashboard includes aggregate metrics across all questions, as well as per-model tables to see fine-grained model results and per-question tables to assess model performance on specific prompts.

We encourage contributions to this benchmark in the form of new models. If you'd like to benchmark a new model, please follow these steps and then submit a PR. Or, feel free to open an issue requesting a new model benchmark.

items.title: So, Don't Overreact But... I'm So Over React
items.author: Bagombeka Job
items.content:
More accurately, I’m over everyone treating it like the only answer. I'm a devout supporter of Web Components and have been preaching the gospel of W3C standards for decades. That guy you always hear muttering "Why use a library when the browser gives you everything you need?"

I have lived through five versions of HTML (2,3,4,XHTML,5) and watched as libraries like jQuery and frameworks like Angular have come and gone from the spotlight. And I'm here now to tell you that you never NEEDED any of them and this has never been more true than it is today.

Custom Elements, Shadow DOM, HTML Templates. These tools provide everything we need to build robust, modern components without relying on heavy abstractions. But still, in 2025, the web development world clings tightly to libraries like React.

The Standard Approach I'll admit that React played a huge role in shaping modern front-end development. Its component model, reactive updates, and rich ecosystem were groundbreaking when they emerged. But React’s reliance on third-party extensions like JSX, its synthetic event system, performance overhead, and tendency to blur styling and logic inside components all fall short of the standards set for the web.

Web Components — by contrast — are a core part of the web standards, embrace the browser’s strengths, benefit from baked in optimizations, and promote separation of concerns. All without the need for build pipelines or runtime libraries. You’re working with the platform, not against it.

Universal by Design Perhaps the most underappreciated strength of Web Components is their inherent interoperability. Build a standards-compliant component, and you can drop it into any toolkit. Whether you’re using Vue, Svelte, whatever the flavor-of-the-year framework is, or just static HTML, Web Components fit right in.

With React, moving components between frameworks often means rewrites, dependency tangles, and awkward integration. Why lock your work into a single ecosystem when the browser already provides a universal language? And don't even get me started on stateful rendering in an environment that was intentionally designed to be stateless.

Well designed Custom Elements can be used completely declaratively, blend seamlessly into Server Side Rendering, can be styled through standard CSS, are naturally encapsulated, and the list of capabilities goes on and on and on...

The State of Things I’m not here to say React is evil or useless. It’s a mature, powerful tool with plenty of successful projects under its belt. But in 2025, when developers continue to reach for it by default, that seems more like a bad habit — a reflex that hasn’t caught up to the evolution of the platform.

For those of us who’ve been building with Web Components for years, the continued use of vendor-specific solutions feels increasingly out of sync with the realities of the web platform. Most of the problems that React once solved have been addressed at the platform level long ago. Web Components have been mature for years. It's time to stop solving today's problems with yesterday’s tools. Imagine if we never stopped using tables for layout just because "That's how we've always done it".

Building for The Future Obviously we all use the tools we know and trust. But sometimes we need to challenge ourselves to re-examine whether those tools are still the best fit for today’s (and tomorrow’s) needs. That's exactly why the standards exist and continue to evolve. And exactly why I will stick with them as I always have. Because one day, React will fade into obscurity just like jQuery while Web Components will live on as long as the web itself.

items.title: Face Recognition Flutter – Key Benefits and Challenges in Mobile Application Development
items.author: Faceplugin
items.content:
Face recognition flutter is transforming the way mobile apps handle user authentication, offering a groundbreaking shift in both security and user experience. As digital transformation accelerates, traditional password-based security methods are becoming outdated.

Face recognition flutter is quickly emerging as a powerful solution, allowing developers to integrate advanced biometric authentication into mobile applications.

By leveraging Flutter’s versatile framework alongside cutting-edge machine learning algorithms, this technology not only ensures robust security but also delivers a seamless, frictionless user experience.

With the rise of data breaches and growing concerns over privacy, mobile security has never been more critical. Face recognition flutter offers an ideal solution by providing an extra layer of security that is both efficient and reliable. Gone are the days of complex passwords or PINs now, a simple glance can unlock access to sensitive information.

In this article, we’ll explore the key benefits and challenges of incorporating face recognition flutter into mobile apps.

Whether you’re a developer looking to enhance your app’s security features or a business seeking to improve user engagement, the integration of face recognition flutter will be crucial in shaping the future of mobile authentication.

What is Face Recognition Flutter? A Game-Changer for Mobile Security

Face recognition flutter integrates facial recognition into mobile apps, boosting both security and user experience. It uses AI algorithms to analyze facial features in real time. Faceplugin provides powerful tools for developers to implement this technology seamlessly across mobile platforms.

Face recognition flutter allows for immediate identity verification, leveraging high-speed processing.

This ensures no delays, giving users a seamless authentication experience.

The technology scans facial features instantly, making authentication faster than ever.Cross-Platform Compatibility Face recognition Flutter works on both Android and iOS through the Flutter framework.

It delivers consistent performance across devices, ensuring a smooth user experience.

Developers can build one solution that supports both platforms, simplifying the process and saving time.

By using face recognition flutter, developers get access to cutting-edge features that enhance security. This technology is critical for mobile apps where user data protection is paramount.

Whether it’s real-time authentication or cross-platform compatibility, face recognition flutter offers reliable, secure, and efficient solutions. It provides a simple yet powerful toolset to ensure your app is both functional and future-proof in mobile security.

items.title: How to pass the AWS AI Practitioner and Machine Learning Associate Exams: Tips and Resources
items.author: Dániel Rozmán
items.content:
AWS certifications are among the most respected credentials in the tech industry, and the AI Practitioner and Machine Learning Associate exams are no exception. These certifications were a big milestone for me, and along the way, I learned a lot about what works — and what doesn’t — when it comes to studying for AWS certifications.

When I first started preparing, I relied on a mix of AWS whitepapers, AWS documentation, and the AWS Skill Builder courses. My company also has a partnership with AWS, so I was able to attend some AWS Partner sessions as part of our collaboration. While these were all helpful resources, I quickly realized that video-based materials weren’t the best fit for me. I found it frustrating to constantly pause videos to take notes, and when I needed to revisit a specific topic later, it was a nightmare trying to scrub through hours of video to find the exact point I needed.

I started looking for written resources that were more structured and easier to reference. At one point, I even bought a book that I thought would help, but it turned out to be a complete rip-off. It was poorly written, clearly just some AI-generated text that wasn’t organized, and it contained incorrect information. That experience made me realize how hard it can be to find reliable study materials.

During my preparation, I ended up piecing together information from all available sources. I started writing my own notes and organizing the material in a way that was easier for me to understand and review. By the time I passed both exams, I realized that the materials I had created could be helpful to others who might be facing the same challenges I did.

So, after passing the exams, I decided to take it a step further. I put in extra effort to refine and expand my notes into professional study guides. My goal was to create resources that thoroughly cover all the topics required to pass the exams, ensuring nothing is left out. I wanted to provide clear explanations, practical examples, and realistic practice questions that closely mirror the actual exam. These guides are designed to be comprehensive, so candidates can rely on them to fully understand the material and feel confident in their preparation.

As a way to give back, I’d like to offer a part of the first chapter of my AWS AI Practitioner study guide for free. It covers the basics of AI, ML, and Deep Learning.

If you find it useful and would like to support me, I’d be incredibly grateful if you considered purchasing the full book. I’ve made the ebook price as affordable as possible so it’s accessible to everyone.

I hope this article has given you valuable insights and a clearer path to preparing for your AWS certifications. Whether you’re just starting your journey or are deep into your studies, remember that persistence and consistent effort are key.

Wishing you the best of luck on your certification exams and your career ahead! If you have any questions or need further guidance, don’t hesitate to reach out — I’m always happy to help.

items.title: Vibe Coding: The Good and the Ugly
items.author: Ricards Taujenis
items.content:
If you have been around the dev block recently, you most likely heard of this new trend "vibe coding" my friend roped me into trying it with Cursor, and let me just tell you I have mixed feelings about it. Here's why.

When it comes to building a new project it feels liberating to vibe yourself to success! Not the same with legacy code however but not impossible.

Real-time feedback and rapid iteration

Cursor(like WindSurf) updates on the fly, making it a lot more faster and you don't have to switch between writing and running as not only it updates scripts but it can as well create new ones!

As you can see from above screenshot for Cursor it at first asked for permission to mkdir and then automatically created schema.sql script.

Vibe coding on fresh project creates less baggage compared to legacy code. In addition it gives you sense of momentum, exploration and blazingly fast builds!  In addition it can do deploys to your Cloud provider if ssh keys are present and manage it in terminal(so not just code).

Great for ideation and brainstorming

Stuck on what tools to use? Postgres or Mongo? RabbitMQ? Kubernetes? Or what API system to use and even language Cursor and other AI IDEs can help with that by leveraging it's deep understanding of all the tools in the disposal. 

Just have to be explicit and scaffold based on my goals, and then gives me full stack with languages, reasoning and tools.

I asked a question of what I am included to build and to follow up with README.md tech stack and architecture tree including language of choice and other tools that would be optimal. ☝️

Even though these AI IDEs have access to your whole repo, they can still duplicate logic and other issues mentioned bellow. Thus extensive monitoring and NOT commiting before checking is a must. 🕵🏻

While code assistant IDEs have access to whole repo, it still sometimes create same functions in different directories. Like I had with my Rust project bellow. 👇

If you pay attention you can see that verify_pin function was created in plugin, service and utils module lvl by Cursor, so when some code doesn't work it can often recreate similar logic elsewhere and forget that there is already something implemented.

This hits hardest in JavaScript and Python. With massive ecosystems, AI assistants often install libraries that don't play well together - or that are outdated.

With React it can install certain dependencies like Supabase client and server side dependencies, same as with TS that are not compatible.

As we all know we can not always use the latest packages and documentation/methods are not available for some packages or are stale with AI not knowing it but just pasting.

Too fast, lack of reasoning

Sometimes it feels too easy to "vibe yourself to success", but often context of repo is misunderstood and explanation of WHY particular approach was taken falls short. Thus it's easy to fall into deep woods when you see 10 scripts generate and instead of reasoning and understand it you just close a blind eye and hope for AI Gods to fix possible issues.

To conclude I must say Cursor undeniably is a game-changer. But just because something becomes 3x easier doesn't mean it can not wrap up "technical debt" in the future, especially if your not fully familiar with the tech like me when it comes to Rust. 

The $20/month price is a bit steep, especially when WindSurf offers a free tier and GitHub Copilot is cheaper. Still, for now, I'm sticking with Cursor.

That said, I'll be more cautious. Just because the assistant offers changes doesn't mean you should accept them blindly. Review them. Test them. Because duplicated functions, unnecessary utils, and mismatched packages can snowball fast.

items.title: 10 Translation Industry Trends: 2025 Outlook
items.author: Ronika K
items.content:
In 2025, the translation industry is at a crossroads, with artificial intelligence (AI) transforming every aspect of how we bridge language gaps. But this year isn’t just about bigger, faster, or more automated solutions.

It’s about smarter solutions that enhance quality, accessibility, and efficiency. Whether you’re a translator, a business leveraging translation services, or a tech enthusiast, the translation industry trends in 2025 will impact how we communicate across borders for years to come.

2025 translation industry trends to anticipate

1. Adaptive AI takes center stage (most impactful translation trend in 2025)

Remember when machine translation was a “good enough” option? That’s not cutting it anymore. Adaptive AI becomes a top translation trend in 2025, ensuring machine translation doesn’t just spit out words but learns in real time.

: In 2024, we emphasized AI’s ability to deliver contextually accurate translations, preserving nuance and cultural sensitivity.

: In 2025, we’re seeing a shift from AI as a tool to AI as a true collaborator. Translators are increasingly leaning on AI for repetitive tasks, allowing them to focus on creativity and complex problem-solving. It’s not just about accuracy—it’s about synergy.

How to harness adaptive AI for translation

Use a translation software platform that combines Dynamic Machine Learning and Translation Memory, refining its output based on user feedback, industry-specific glossaries, and even brand tone of voice. The result? Personalized translations that feel crafted, not computed.

Businesses want translations that align with their brand voice and industry jargon without spending weeks on manual edits. Adaptive AI promises faster delivery and higher accuracy, making it a game-changer for high-volume translation needs.

2. Ethical AI and data privacy

AI is undeniably powerful—but with great power comes great responsibility. As more organizations adopt AI translation tools, the conversation around ethical AI and data privacy is heating up as one of the top translation industry trends.

Clients want assurances that their sensitive documents won’t be used to train third-party models. Meanwhile, governments are cracking down on AI misuse with stricter regulations around data handling and transparency. See how Pairaphrase’s responsible AI policy empowers organizations to harness AI in their translation process with peace of mind.

Translation providers will prioritize end-to-end encryption and give users greater control over how their data is stored and used. Ethical AI won’t just be a buzzword—it’ll be a requirement for staying competitive.

3. Real-time multilingual communication

Real-time translation tools aren’t new, but in 2025, they’re leveling up. AI is making it possible to translate entire conversations instantly, and not just in text form. Speech-to-speech translation is advancing rapidly, offering near-perfect voice synthesis that sounds like the speaker.

: Real-time translation services made strides in 2024, helping organizations transcend language barriers in global communication.

: 2025 takes this trend a step further, with AI-driven real-time speech-to-speech translation and voice synthesis, enabling seamless multilingual conversations across industries.

Imagine a boardroom where participants speak their native languages and hear real-time translations in their own voice tones. That’s not a distant future—it’s happening now.

With hybrid workplaces and global teams, seamless multilingual communication tools will be essential. Look out for apps and platforms offering voice, video, and chat translation, all in real time.

4. Hyper-localization with a global mindset

Translation isn’t just about words—it’s about context and maintaining humanity. Hyper-localization has become a must-have strategy for brands entering international markets. But in 2025, it goes deeper than swapping idioms or adjusting measurements.

AI tools are now analyzing regional cultural nuances, slang, and even societal trends to craft translations that truly resonate. This year, hyper-localization will extend beyond marketing into areas like eLearning, customer support, and UX design.

How it compares to last year’s translation trend projections

Last Year: AI-driven Localization 2.0 in 2024 emphasized tailoring content to resonate deeply with target audiences.

This Year: Hyper-localization in 2025 expands this concept, incorporating cultural trends, regional slang, and societal nuances for content that feels personal and profoundly relevant.

Why this trend is critical

Companies that fail to localize at this level risk alienating their audiences. In a hyper-connected world, one-size-fits-all messaging just won’t cut it.

5. AI-augmented human translation

Despite the rapid evolution of AI, one fact remains: machines can’t 100% replicate human creativity and cultural intuition. In 2025, expect this language industry trend of AI augmenting—rather than replacing—human translators.

Think of AI as a co-pilot

It handles repetitive tasks like formatting and terminology management while human experts focus on nuanced edits and stylistic flair. In some integrations, like a ChatGPT integration within translation software, AI becomes your direct translation assistant. This hybrid model will dominate workflows, combining speed with authenticity.

Pro tip: If you’re a translator, embrace AI tools that streamline your work without compromising quality. Your ability to combine human insight with AI efficiency will set you apart.

6. The rise of voice-to-text translation

As voice interfaces grow in popularity, so does the need for real-time voice-to-text translation. From virtual assistants to customer support chatbots, this translation trend is driven by demand for accessible and multilingual voice-based interactions.

: Speech-to-text and speech-to-speech technologies were also highlighted in 2024 as tools for inclusivity and accessibility.

: In 2025, the focus is on integrating voice-to-text translation into enterprise workflows and consumer applications, streamlining communication for diverse audiences.

Why this 2025 trend matters

Organizations can use this technology to enhance customer experience, making interactions smoother across language barriers.

7. Specialized AI for niche industries

AI is moving beyond generic translation to cater further into niche markets such as legal, medical, and technical fields. These specialized systems are trained with industry-specific terminology, ensuring higher accuracy, quality, and relevance for critical documents.

Startups and established companies alike are racing to create tailored AI solutions for highly regulated or jargon-heavy industries.

8. The human touch: translation for brand identity

While AI handles speed and scalability, businesses are doubling down on human translation for high-stakes projects like marketing campaigns and creative content. The focus is on maintaining brand voice and cultural resonance.

AI and human collaboration will thrive, with humans taking the lead in areas where marketing strategy, emotional nuance and cultural context are paramount.

9. Continuous translation in the SaaS world

As SaaS platforms expand globally, continuous translation workflows are becoming essential. This localization trend involves integrating translation directly into product development, ensuring multilingual updates are released simultaneously with other features.

Why this trend is transformative

It allows global users to experience new features without delay, improving user satisfaction and retention.

10. AI in quality assurance

AI is stepping in to enhance quality assurance (QA) in translations. Automated QA tools now identify inconsistencies, missing translations, and formatting errors, reducing time spent on manual reviews.

: Collaborative translation workflows in 2024 spotlighted the partnership between AI and humans in maintaining quality.

: AI is stepping into the quality assurance role directly in 2025, using machine learning to identify errors, inconsistencies, and formatting issues before humans even get involved.

For translators and their organizations, integrating these tools can speed up delivery timelines and improve client and/or customer satisfaction.

2025 is shaping up to be a transformative year for the translation industry. From adaptive AI to hyper-localization, the translation trends we’re seeing reflect a deeper integration of technology with human expertise. Staying ahead of these developments isn’t just about adopting new tools—it’s about understanding the changing landscape of communication itself.

items.title: How AEPS B2B Software Is Revolutionizing Rural Banking for Retailers and Distributors
items.author: ankit vj
items.content:
India’s rural economy is transforming fast with the help of digital banking solutions. One of the most powerful tools driving this change is AEPS B2B Software (Aadhaar Enabled Payment System for Business-to-Business use). This technology is making it easier for local retailers, distributors, and agents to offer banking services in places where traditional banks are hard to reach.

Thanks to innovative fintech companies like noblewebstudio, is now helping expand financial services across villages and small towns. In this article, we’ll explore how AEPS is revolutionizing rural banking for retailers and distributors in Bharat.

What Is AEPS B2B Software?

AEPS B2B software allows users to perform basic banking transactions such as:

Aadhaar-to-Aadhaar fund transfers

All of this is done using biometric authentication linked to the Aadhaar number. With AEPS software, a local retailer can act as a mini bank, offering essential services to customers using just their fingerprint and Aadhaar number.

How It Helps Retailers and Distributors

1. Boosts Income Through Commission

Retailers and agents earn a commission on every AEPS transaction. For rural shopkeepers, this is a great way to increase their monthly earnings while helping their community. Distributors managing multiple agents can also earn through platform usage and transaction volume.

2. Turns Local Shops Into Digital Banks

With AEPS B2B software, a local store can offer banking services like cash withdrawal and balance checks. This reduces the need for villagers to travel far to access a bank or ATM.

3. Easy to Use, No Tech Skills Required

One of the reasons AEPS is spreading so fast is that it doesn’t require any complex setup. A fingerprint scanner, a smartphone or desktop, and a reliable AEPS software provider like noblewebstudio are all that’s needed.

Why AEPS B2B Software Matters in Rural Banking

AEPS is a key player in India’s financial inclusion mission. It enables banking for the unbanked by offering doorstep services through retailers. Even people without smartphones or internet access can use AEPS.

Secure and Fast Transactions

Every transaction is verified using biometric data, making it extremely secure. AEPS systems also ensure real-time settlements, so retailers get credited quickly.

Traditional bank branches have working hours and are often far from rural areas. With AEPS software, banking services are available anytime a shop is open, even on weekends.

Why Choose noblewebstudio for AEPS B2B Software?

Noblewebstudio is a trusted AEPS service provider in India. They offer:

Multi-user management (retailer/distributor hierarchy)

API and white-label solutions

Retailers and distributors using noblewebstudio’s platform get full control, branding options, and dedicated customer support—making it a complete AEPS business solution.

AEPS in Action: A Real-Life Example

Imagine a small village with no nearby bank branch. The local grocery store owner signs up as an AEPS retailer using noblewebstudio's software. Now, villagers can come to this shop to withdraw money from their bank accounts, check balances, or receive government benefits—all through Aadhaar authentication. The shop owner earns with every transaction, and the village no longer faces banking hardships.

AEPS B2B software is not just a tool—it’s a financial revolution for rural India. It empowers retailers and distributors, increases income opportunities, and most importantly, brings essential banking services closer to people who need them the most.

With reliable solutions from providers like noblewebstudio, AEPS is truly transforming the way rural India banks. Whether you’re a small retailer or a distributor managing a network of agents, now is the time to adopt AEPS and be part of the digital banking movement.
